methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $13M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Transportation (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 5 same-sector peers exist in Transportation, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

private.name: REGENT Craft
private.rationale: Same sector (Transportation), same stage (Series A), post-money $270M

data.narrative:
Sarla Aviation is a Series A transportation company that has raised $13M to date. The broader Transportation sector includes eight private comparables, though narrow peer filtering—targeting same-stage, adjacent-stage, or similar-valuation-tier companies—yielded insufficient data for reliable peer-median analysis. No public Transportation peers with relevant characteristics were identified.

Given the single applicable triangulation method, Stage-Progression analysis applies a 2.0x step-up multiplier to the $13M total raised proxy, implying a post-money valuation of $26M. This reflects typical venture progression from Series A to the next financing stage. The peer-median approach was deemed inapplicable due to insufficient comparables meeting narrow selection criteria; the five Transportation comps in the broader universe (including REGENT Craft, Rivian, The Boring Company, Einride, and Harbinger) operate at materially different stages or valuation tiers, making their data non-informative for this stage-specific analysis.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $22M (low) to $30M (high), with a median of $26M. This range reflects the single quantitative method applied and incorporates reasonable variance around the step-up assumption. Key uncertainties include the absence of narrow-peer benchmarking, unit economics validity, and execution risk at Series A. Validation of the 2.0x multiplier assumption against actual stage transitions in early-stage transportation would materially strengthen confidence in this range.
